q: A diatomic gas with rigid molecules does <math><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of work when expanded at constant pressure. What would be the heat energy absorbed by the gas, in this process?
question: A diatomic gas with rigid molecules does <math><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of work when expanded at constant pressure. What would be the heat energy absorbed by the gas, in this process?
solution:
Cp=72R (for diatomic gas)
ΔQ=nCpΔT ∴Isobaric process
ΔW=nRΔT=10 J
Given
∴ΔQ=n×72RΔT=72×10=35 J nRΔT=10 J

subject: Physics
q: A metal ball of mass $0.1 \mathrm{~kg}$ is heated upto $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and dropped into a vessel of heat capacity $800 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}$ and containing $0.5 \mathrm{~kg}$ water. The initial temperature of water and vessel is $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the approximate percentage increment in the temperature of the water? [Specific Heat Capacities of water and metal are, respectively, $4200 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-}$ ${ }^{1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ and $400 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ ]
question: A metal ball of mass $0.1 \mathrm{~kg}$ is heated upto $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and dropped into a vessel of heat capacity $800 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}$ and containing $0.5 \mathrm{~kg}$ water. The initial temperature of water and vessel is $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the approximate percentage increment in the temperature of the water? [Specific Heat Capacities of water and metal are, respectively, $4200 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-}$ ${ }^{1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ and $400 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ ]
solution:
Assume final temperature $=\mathrm{T}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ Heat lass $=$ Heat gain $=\mathrm{ms} \Delta \mathrm{T}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{B}} \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{w}} \mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{w}} \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{w}}$

$0.1 \times 400 \times(500-\mathrm{T})$

$\quad=0.5 \times 4200 \times(\mathrm{T}-30)+800(\mathrm{~T}-30)$

$\Rightarrow 40(500-\mathrm{T})=(\mathrm{T}-30)(2100+800)$

$\Rightarrow 20000-40 \mathrm{~T}=2900 \mathrm{~T}-30 \times 2900$

$\Rightarrow 20000+30 \times 2900=\mathrm{T}(2940)$

$\mathrm{T}=30.4^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}} \times 100=\frac{6.4}{30} \times 100=21 \%$

so the closest answer is $20 \%$.

question: The number of molecules in one litre of an ideal gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> atmospheric pressure with mean kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> per molecule is:
solution:
KE=32kT
PV=NNART
N=PVkT
=N=1.5×1011

subject: Physics
q: A massless spring <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>800</mn><mtext> </mtext><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> attached with a mass <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>500</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is completely immersed in <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water. The spring is stretched by <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and released so that it starts vibrating. What would be the order of magnitude of the change in the temperature of water when the vibrations stop completely? (Assume that the water container and spring receive negligible heat and specific heat of mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>400</mn><mi> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> specific heat of water <math><mo>=</mo><mn>4184</mn><mtext> </mtext><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> )
question: A massless spring <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>800</mn><mtext> </mtext><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> attached with a mass <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>500</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is completely immersed in <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water. The spring is stretched by <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and released so that it starts vibrating. What would be the order of magnitude of the change in the temperature of water when the vibrations stop completely? (Assume that the water container and spring receive negligible heat and specific heat of mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>400</mn><mi> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> specific heat of water <math><mo>=</mo><mn>4184</mn><mtext> </mtext><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> )
solution:
According to the law of conservation of energy,
12kx2=m1s1+m2s2×ΔT
⇒ΔT=12×800×2×10-220.5×400+1×4184=16×10-24384 =3.65×10-5

q: The average kinetic energy of a molecule of the gas is
question: The average kinetic energy of a molecule of the gas is
options: dependent on the nature of the gas
solution:
The average kinetic energy of a molecule of the gas  =32RT.

Average kinetic energy of one molecule of gas =32kT    ( k=RNA)

Where k is Boltzmann constant and T is absolute temperature.

So, from above relation we can say average kinetic energy of molecule is directly proportional to absolute temperature.

subject: Physics
q: A gas mixture consists of 3 moles of oxygen and 5 moles of argon at temperature T. Considering only translational and rotational modes, the total internal energy of the system is
question: A gas mixture consists of 3 moles of oxygen and 5 moles of argon at temperature T. Considering only translational and rotational modes, the total internal energy of the system is
solution:
$U=\frac{f_{1}}{2} n_{1} R T+\frac{f_{2}}{2} n_{2} R T$

Considering translational and rotational modes, degrees of freedom $\mathrm{f}_{1}=5$ and $\mathrm{f}_{2}=3$ $\therefore \mathrm{u}=\frac{5}{2}(3 \mathrm{RT})+\frac{3}{2} \times 5 \mathrm{RT}$

$\mathrm{U}=15 \mathrm{RT}$

subject: Physics
q:
Which statements are correct about degrees of freedom?

A. A molecule with n degrees of freedom has n2 different ways of storing energy.

B. Each degree of freedom is associated with 12RT average energy per mole.

C. A monoatomic gas molecule has 1 rotational degree of freedom where as diatomic molecule has 2 rotational degrees of freedom

D. CH4 has a total to 6 degrees of freedom.

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
question:
Which statements are correct about degrees of freedom?

A. A molecule with n degrees of freedom has n2 different ways of storing energy.

B. Each degree of freedom is associated with 12RT average energy per mole.

C. A monoatomic gas molecule has 1 rotational degree of freedom where as diatomic molecule has 2 rotational degrees of freedom

D. CH4 has a total to 6 degrees of freedom.

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
solution:
According to the equipartition of energy, each degree of freedom carries 12RT energy per mole and total energy per mole of a gas having n degrees of freedom is 12nRT. Therefore, statement A is wrong and statement B is correct.

A monoatomic molecule only has translational degrees of freedom and does not have rotational degree of freedom. For a polyatomic model there are three rotational degrees of freedom and three translational degrees of freedom. Therefore, statement C is incorrect and statement D is correct.

subject: Physics
q: Consider a gas of triatomic molecules. The molecules  are assumed to be triangular and made of massless rigid rods whose vertices are occupied by atoms. The internal energy of a mole of the gas at temperature T is:
question: Consider a gas of triatomic molecules. The molecules  are assumed to be triangular and made of massless rigid rods whose vertices are occupied by atoms. The internal energy of a mole of the gas at temperature T is:
solution:
The formula of internal energy of a gas is given by

U=f2RT

Here R is the gas constant and T is temperature.

R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1

For the given configuration of triatomic molecules, the degree of freedom f=6

⇒ U=62RT=3RT

q: A mixture of hydrogen and oxygen has volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>k</mi><mi>Pa</mi></math> and mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>76</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The ratio of masses of oxygen to hydrogen will be:
question: A mixture of hydrogen and oxygen has volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>k</mi><mi>Pa</mi></math> and mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>76</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The ratio of masses of oxygen to hydrogen will be:
solution:
A number of moles of, O2=m132, the number of moles of, H2=m22. Using the ideal gas equation, PV=m132+m22253×300, 
400×103×500×10-6=m132+m2225×100⇒2×102=m1m2×132+12m2×25×100
⇒225=m1m2×132+12m2  ...(1)   
⇒m1+m2=0.76
⇒m21+m1m2=76100   ...(2) 
(1) divided by (2) ; m1m2×132+12m1m2+1=225×10076
Let assume, 
⇒m1m2=x⇒x+1632x+1=876⇒ 76x+76×16=8×32x+8×32⇒76x+1216=256x+256
960=180x⇒x=9618=163

q: An unknown metal of mass <math><mn>192</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>g</mi></math> heated to a temperature of <math><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi>C</mi></math> was immersed into a brass calorimeter of mass <math><mn>128</mn><mi> </mi><mi>g</mi></math> containing <math><mn>240</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>g</mi></math> of water at a temperature of <math><msup><mrow><mn>8.4</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Calculate the specific heat of the unknown metal if water temperature stabilizes at <math><msup><mrow><mn>21.5</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> <math><mo>(</mo></math> Specific heat of brass is <math><mn>394</mn><mi>J</mi><mtext> </mtext><mi>k</mi><msup><mrow><mi>g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math>
question: An unknown metal of mass <math><mn>192</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>g</mi></math> heated to a temperature of <math><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi>C</mi></math> was immersed into a brass calorimeter of mass <math><mn>128</mn><mi> </mi><mi>g</mi></math> containing <math><mn>240</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>g</mi></math> of water at a temperature of <math><msup><mrow><mn>8.4</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Calculate the specific heat of the unknown metal if water temperature stabilizes at <math><msup><mrow><mn>21.5</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> <math><mo>(</mo></math> Specific heat of brass is <math><mn>394</mn><mi>J</mi><mtext> </mtext><mi>k</mi><msup><mrow><mi>g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
Conservation of energy:

msΔT=mbsb+mwswΔTw

192×10-3s100-21.5=128×394+240×4182×10-3×21.5-8.4

s=50432+100368013.115072=916.19≃916 J kg-1 K-1

q: Which of the following shows the correct relationship between the pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>'</mo></math> and density <math><mi>ρ</mi></math> of an ideal gas at constant temperature ?
question: Which of the following shows the correct relationship between the pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>'</mo></math> and density <math><mi>ρ</mi></math> of an ideal gas at constant temperature ?
solution:
ρ = PM RT

P = ρ RT M

P ∝ ρ

subject: Physics
q:
A cylindrical container of volume 4.0×10-3 m3 contains one mole of hydrogen and two moles of carbon dioxide. Assume the temperature of the mixture is 400 K. The pressure of the mixture of gases is :
[Take gas constant as 8.3 J mol-1 K-1
question:
A cylindrical container of volume 4.0×10-3 m3 contains one mole of hydrogen and two moles of carbon dioxide. Assume the temperature of the mixture is 400 K. The pressure of the mixture of gases is :
[Take gas constant as 8.3 J mol-1 K-1
solution:
For gas A

PAV=nART

PA=(1)RTV

for gas B

PBV=nBRT

PB=(2)RTV

Now according to the Dalton's law of mixture,

P=PA+PB

=3RTV=3×8.314(400)4×10-3=24.9×105 Pa

subject: Physics
q: When a gas filled in a closed vessel is heated by raising the temperature by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, its pressure increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>%</mo></math> The initial temperature of the gas is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math>.
question: When a gas filled in a closed vessel is heated by raising the temperature by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, its pressure increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>%</mo></math> The initial temperature of the gas is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math>.
solution:
From ideal gas equation, PV=nRT, where, n is amount of substance, R is ideal gas constant and V is volume.

In closed vessel volume remains constant.

So, dPP×100=dTT×100

0.4=1T×100

⇒T=250 K

q: On the basis of kinetic theory of gases, the gas exerts pressure because its molecules:
question: On the basis of kinetic theory of gases, the gas exerts pressure because its molecules:
options: continuously stick to the walls of container.
suffer change in momentum when impinge on the walls of container.
continuously lose their energy till it reaches wall.
are attracted by the walls of container.
solution: From the assumption of KTG, the molecules of gas collide with the walls and suffers momentum change which results in force on the wall and hence pressure.

subject: Physics
q:
0.056 kg of Nitrogen is enclosed in a vessel at a temperature of 127°C. The amount of heat required to double the speed of its molecules is_____ kcal.
(Take R=2 cal mole K-1-1 )
question:
0.056 kg of Nitrogen is enclosed in a vessel at a temperature of 127°C. The amount of heat required to double the speed of its molecules is_____ kcal.
(Take R=2 cal mole K-1-1 )
solution:
The RMS speed of the molecule is given by vRMS=3RTM. Therefore, to double the speed the temperature must become 4 times.

Therefore, the change in the temperature is ∆T=3T0.

Now the process is isochoric (enclosed vessel) therefore, the heat required will be,

H=nCv∆T⇒H=5628×52R×3×273+127=12000 cal=12 kcal

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of specific heats <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>P</mi></msub><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>V</mi></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> in terms of degree of freedom <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>f</mi></mfenced></math> is given by :
question: The ratio of specific heats <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>P</mi></msub><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>V</mi></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> in terms of degree of freedom <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>f</mi></mfenced></math> is given by :
solution:
From Mayer's relation CP=CV+R and CV=f2R

Ratio of specific heat,

γ=CPCV=1+Rf2R=1+2f

subject: Physics
q: A flask contains argon and oxygen in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math> in mass and the mixture is kept at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The ratio of their average kinetic energy per molecule respectively
question: A flask contains argon and oxygen in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math> in mass and the mixture is kept at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The ratio of their average kinetic energy per molecule respectively
solution:
Given here, mArmO=32.

As argon is monoatomic gas, so it has three degree of freedom f and oxygen is a diatomic gas, so its degree of freedom is five.

We know that average kinetic energy per molecule is given by KE=f2kT, where, k is Boltzmann's constant and T is temperature.

Here, T=27°C is fixed for both gas.

So, KEArKEO=32kT52kT=35

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I :The average momentum of a molecule in a sample of an ideal gas depends on temperature.

Statement II : The rms speed of oxygen molecules in a gas is v. If the temperature is doubled and the oxygen molecules dissociate into oxygen atoms, the rms speed will become 2v.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I :The average momentum of a molecule in a sample of an ideal gas depends on temperature.

Statement II : The rms speed of oxygen molecules in a gas is v. If the temperature is doubled and the oxygen molecules dissociate into oxygen atoms, the rms speed will become 2v.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Momentum is a vector quantity and average momentum of molecules of a sample is zero due to random motion.

Pavg =0

RMS speed of a molecule is given by

vrms=3RTM

Now the temperature is doubled Tnew =2T and due to dissociation Mnew=M2.

⇒vnew v=2TM2TM

⇒vnew=2v

subject: Physics
q: The specific heats, <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow></msub></math> of a gas of diatomic molecules, <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> are given (in units of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> ) by <math><mn>29</mn></math> and <math><mn>22</mn><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. Another gas of diatomic molecules, <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> has the corresponding values <math><mn>30</mn></math> and <math><mn>21</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If they are treated as ideal gases, then:
question: The specific heats, <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow></msub></math> of a gas of diatomic molecules, <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> are given (in units of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> ) by <math><mn>29</mn></math> and <math><mn>22</mn><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. Another gas of diatomic molecules, <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> has the corresponding values <math><mn>30</mn></math> and <math><mn>21</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If they are treated as ideal gases, then:
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> has one vibrational mode and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> has two
<math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> has a vibrational mode but <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> has none.
Both <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> have a vibrational mode each.
<math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is rigid but <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> has a vibrational mode.
solution:
For A :
R=Cp-Cv=29-22=7
Cv=fR2
⇒22=f×72
⇒f=447=6.3
⇒f≈6

(5 rotational + translational, 1 vibrational)


For B :
R=Cp-CV=30-21=9
Cv=fR2=21
⇒f=21×29=429=4.66≃5
∴ f≃5

 (5 rotational + transitional, 0 vibrational)

subject: Physics
q: To raise the temperature of a certain mass of gas by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at a constant pressure,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn></math> calories of heat is required. When the same mass of gas is cooled by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at constant volume,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn></math> calories of heat is released. How many degrees of freedom does each molecule of this gas have (assume gas to be ideal)?
question: To raise the temperature of a certain mass of gas by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at a constant pressure,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn></math> calories of heat is required. When the same mass of gas is cooled by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at constant volume,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn></math> calories of heat is released. How many degrees of freedom does each molecule of this gas have (assume gas to be ideal)?
solution:
At constant pressure :

∆Q=nCP∆T ⇒   160=nCP.50  .....(1)

At constant volume

∆Q=nCv∆T ⇒  240=nCv.100......(2)

Equation (1) divided by (2)

160240=CPCV·50100

CPCV=43=1+2f

f=6

subject: Physics
q:
The mean free path of a molecule of diameter $5 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$ at the temperature $41^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and pressure $1.38 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$, is given as $\_\_\_\_$ m.
(Given $k_{B}=1.38 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$).
question:
The mean free path of a molecule of diameter $5 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$ at the temperature $41^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and pressure $1.38 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$, is given as $\_\_\_\_$ m.
(Given $k_{B}=1.38 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$).
solution:
Mean free path is calculated using $\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P}$
Given: $d = 5 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $T = 41°C = 314$ K, $P = 1.38 \times 10^5$ Pa, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
$\lambda = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 314}{\sqrt{2} \times \pi \times (5 \times 10^{-10})^2 \times 1.38 \times 10^5}$
$= \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 314}{\sqrt{2} \times \pi \times 25 \times 10^{-20} \times 1.38 \times 10^5}$
Numerator: $433.32 \times 10^{-23}$
Denominator: $1.414 \times 3.14159 \times 34.5 \times 10^{-15} = 153.18 \times 10^{-15}$
$\lambda = \frac{433.32 \times 10^{-23}}{153.18 \times 10^{-15}} = 2.83 \times 10^{-8}$ m = $2\sqrt{2} \times 10^{-8}$ m

subject: Physics
q: When heat <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></math> is supplied to a diatomic gas of rigid molecules, at constant volume its temperature increases by <math><mi> </mi><mo>∆</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> . The heat required to produce the same change in temperature, at a constant pressure is:
question: When heat <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></math> is supplied to a diatomic gas of rigid molecules, at constant volume its temperature increases by <math><mi> </mi><mo>∆</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> . The heat required to produce the same change in temperature, at a constant pressure is:
solution:
For diatomic gas CP=72R
CV=52R
Heat supplied at constant volume Q=nCVΔT
Heat supplied at constant pressure Q'=nCPΔT
QQ'=CVCP
Q'=QCPCV=75Q

subject: Physics
q: For a particular ideal gas which of the following graphs represents the variation of mean squared velocity of the gas molecules with temperature?
question: For a particular ideal gas which of the following graphs represents the variation of mean squared velocity of the gas molecules with temperature?
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{ms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}} \\
& \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{rms}}^2=3 \mathrm{RT} / \mathrm{M}
\end{aligned}$

Hence we can conclude that $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}^2$ is directly proportional to temperature
$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}$
$\Rightarrow$ Graph will be straight line

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : The mean free path of gas molecules is inversely proportional to square of molecular diameter.
Statement (II) : Average kinetic energy of gas molecules is directly proportional to absolute temperature of gas.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : The mean free path of gas molecules is inversely proportional to square of molecular diameter.
Statement (II) : Average kinetic energy of gas molecules is directly proportional to absolute temperature of gas.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\sqrt{2} \pi \mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{P}} \\ & \mathrm{KE}=\frac{\mathrm{f}}{2} \mathrm{nRT}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The mean free path and the average speed of oxygen molecules at 300 K and 1 atm are $3 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$ and $600 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ respectively. Find the frequency of its collisions.
question: The mean free path and the average speed of oxygen molecules at 300 K and 1 atm are $3 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$ and $600 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ respectively. Find the frequency of its collisions.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Frequency }=\frac{1}{T}=\frac{V_{\text {avg }}}{\lambda} \\ & =\frac{600}{3 \times 157}=2 \times 10^9 \mathrm{sec}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
